title: SWEEP-TEST: handleJoin - no test for invite-only campfire rejection
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleJoin()

handleJoin serves any join request without checking whether the campfire join_protocol is open or invite-only. The join_protocol field exists in the JoinResponse but is not enforced server-side. Any agent with a valid identity can call POST /campfire/{id}/join and receive the encrypted private key if the key provider is set.

Missing tests:
- invite-only campfire: handleJoin should reject joiners not pre-approved (currently no enforcement exists)
- No key provider set: returns 501 (tested indirectly but no explicit test)
- Invalid EphemeralX25519Pub hex: returns 400 (not tested)
- campfire membership not found in store: returns 404 (not tested)
- Join request with no EphemeralX25519Pub: JoinResponse.EncryptedPrivKey should be nil (not tested)

The invite-only gap is P1: it means the p2p-http transport ignores the join_protocol and distributes the campfire private key to any requester who can sign an HTTP request.

Done: tests for each missing path; join_protocol enforcement added or explicitly documented as out-of-scope with a tracking bead.

title: SWEEP-BUG: runNATPoll one-shot mode only retries until peerIdx wraps, may miss error
SWEEP-SEC: handleDeliver and handleSync accept messages from any valid Ed25519 signer (no membership check)
description:
Severity: CRITICAL

handleDeliver (POST /campfire/{id}/deliver) and handleSync (GET /campfire/{id}/sync) only verify that the sender has a valid Ed25519 keypair — they do NOT check whether the sender is a member of the campfire.

Anyone who generates a valid Ed25519 keypair can:
- Deliver arbitrary messages to any campfire they know the ID of
- Read (sync) all messages from any campfire they know the ID of

Only handlePoll performs a membership check (lines 267-283 in handler.go).

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go
- handleDeliver: lines 121-177 — signature verified but no membership check
- handleSync: lines 181-233 — signature verified but no membership check
- handlePoll: lines 246-377 — DOES perform membership check (only endpoint that does)

Impact: Broken access control. Any actor knowing the campfire ID can inject messages and read all history. The campfire ID (Ed25519 public key hex) is discoverable via beacons and membership events.

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-BUG: GetMessageByPrefix reads extra rows after finding ambiguity
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go, GetMessageByPrefix (line 266-295)

The query fetches all matching rows from the DB (no LIMIT clause), but the ambiguity check reads up to len(matches) > 1 and returns an error. If there are 1000 matches, it reads all 1000 rows before returning the error. 

Worse, when 2+ matches are found, the error is returned before rows.Close() can be called via defer. Actually, rows.Close() IS deferred, so that part is OK. But the real issue is: the query uses `ORDER BY id` with no `LIMIT 2`, so for a short prefix matching many messages, the DB scans and returns all matches. The correct fix is to use `LIMIT 2` in the query so the DB stops after finding 2 matches.

Impact: performance degradation under high message volumes when a user provides a very short prefix that matches many messages. Not a correctness bug but a resource waste issue.

title: SWEEP-BUG: PutFile in github client reads then writes without atomic check - TOCTOU race
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/client.go, PutFile (line 279-328)

PutFile first GETs the file to retrieve its SHA (line 285-297), then PUTs the file with the SHA (line 305-327). Between the GET and the PUT, another process could update the file on GitHub. The PUT would then fail with a 409 Conflict (SHA mismatch).

The code does not handle this case:
  if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusCreated && resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
      b, _ := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
      return fmt.Errorf("put file: HTTP %d: %s", resp.StatusCode, b)
  }

A 409 from GitHub when two agents simultaneously publish beacons would surface as an opaque error. There is no retry logic.

Additionally, the getResp.Body is read only when StatusCode == 200 (line 292-295), but for other status codes (e.g. 404 meaning file does not exist yet, which is valid for new files), the body is not read. This is fine since existingSHA stays empty ("") in that case.

However, getResp.Body.Close() on line 297 is called unconditionally, which is correct.

The TOCTOU window is inherent in GitHub's API and is typically handled with retry-on-conflict logic. Missing retry means concurrent beacon updates silently fail.

title: No mechanism to change an existing member's role
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.2.2 review.

admit.go rejects members who are already present ("already a member"). There is no cf admit --role for existing members and no cf member role-set command. To change a member role you must directly edit the CBOR file in the transport directory.

This is a critical UX gap for the observer/writer/full role system: you can assign a role at admit time, but you cannot promote or demote a member afterward.

Done condition: A new command (or --force flag on admit) that updates the Role field of an existing member record in the transport directory. Tests verify role upgrade/downgrade persists through CBOR round-trip.

Parent: workspace-o3l.2
close_reason: Deferred: role change mechanism is a UX enhancement, not a bug.

title: SWEEP-SEC: evict command role check is local-only; any agent can claim creator role by manipulating local store
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

The evict command (evict.go line 65) checks m.Role == "creator" against the local SQLite store. The role field is set locally when the user creates or joins a campfire — it is not cryptographically bound to any campfire-signed credential.

Attack:
1. Agent A joins campfire C (gets role "full")
2. Agent A directly edits ~/.campfire/store.db: UPDATE campfire_memberships SET role = "creator" WHERE campfire_id = ?
3. Agent A runs `cf evict C <victimPubkey>` — eviction proceeds
4. Agent A sends rekey notifications to all remaining peers, replacing their campfire key

This is feasible because:
- SQLite DB is at a known path (~/.campfire/store.db)
- No integrity protection on the store
- No server-side authorization check in handleRekey (see workspace-j4j)

More broadly: the "creator" role is self-asserted via local state — there is no mechanism for other nodes to verify that an eviction request came from the legitimate creator.

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go lines 57-66

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleSign has no authorization check — any valid signer can participate in FROST signing sessions
description:
Severity: HIGH

POST /campfire/{id}/sign verifies the request signature (valid Ed25519 keypair) but does NOT verify that the sender is a member of the campfire or has permission to initiate/participate in threshold signing sessions.

Attack:
1. Attacker learns a threshold campfire ID
2. Attacker sends a round-1 sign request — creates a signing session on the co-signer
3. Attacker can attempt to complete threshold signatures on arbitrary messages
4. Session state accumulates in memory (map keyed by sessionID) — no limit on concurrent sessions, DoS via session exhaustion

Additionally, the sessionID is attacker-supplied (arbitrary string). A malicious co-signer could collide session IDs with legitimate sessions, potentially corrupting signing state.

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleSign() lines 610-746

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: Bug: one-shot read/dag cursor advances on filtered messages, causing re-display
description:
In cmd/cf/cmd/read.go one-shot mode (lines 421-496), allMessages is reassigned to the filtered result on line 422 before cursor advancement on lines 486-496. This means when using --tag or --sender filters, filtered-out messages do not advance the cursor, so they re-appear on the next cf read invocation. The --follow mode correctly separates pre-filter (newMessages) and post-filter (filteredMessages) and advances cursors based on pre-filter data (lines 361-389).

Same bug exists in cmd/cf/cmd/dag.go: line 68 filters, then lines 77-85 advance cursor on filtered result.

Fix: In one-shot read, save the pre-filter messages, apply filterMessages for display, but advance cursor based on the pre-filter set. Same for dag.go.
close_reason: Fixed in c25edc6. Regression test added in 1e97f93.

title: SWEEP-AP: inline migration ALTER TABLE statements bypass schema versioning
description:
pkg/store/store.go Open() function contains inline ALTER TABLE migrations at lines 113-116:

```go
db.Exec("ALTER TABLE messages ADD COLUMN instance TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT ''") //nolint:errcheck
db.Exec("ALTER TABLE campfire_memberships ADD COLUMN description TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT ''") //nolint:errcheck
```

These are silently ignored migrations (errors suppressed with nolint). Problems:
1. There is no schema version tracking - if a migration is needed in the future, there is no record of which migrations have run.
2. The nolint comments hide the fact that these migrations will fail on every subsequent open (SQLite returns an error when a column already exists). The failure is expected but is invisible.
3. New migrations added to this list will silently fail if they conflict, with no developer feedback.

A proper migration strategy (even a simple version integer in a metadata table) or use of a migrations library would make this explicit and safe.

Severity: MEDIUM - fragile migration path; first sign of trouble is silent data corruption when a new migration conflicts with existing columns.

title: SWEEP-BUG: isCompactionMsg uses strings.Contains vs HasTag in store - inconsistent tag detection
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go line 239-241 vs pkg/store/store.go line 732

Two different implementations detect campfire:compact tags:
- compact.go isCompactionMsg: strings.Contains(m.Tags, "\"campfire:compact\"")  
- store.go isCompactionEvent: HasTag(rec.Tags, "campfire:compact") via JSON parse

These are used in different paths: isCompactionMsg is the WRITE path (determining what to supersede when creating a compaction event), isCompactionEvent is the READ path (filtering superseded messages).

If a future tag like "campfire:compact-v2" is introduced, strings.Contains would falsely match it (the JSON "campfire:compact-v2" does not contain the literal "campfire:compact" with trailing quote, so actually safe for v2 case). The real danger is a tag value ending in campfire:compact such as "new-campfire:compact" stored as JSON gives ["new-campfire:compact"] which does contain the substring "campfire:compact" but NOT the search string "\"campfire:compact\"" (the leading quote differs).

The critical actual bug: these two implementations will diverge if they are ever updated independently, and since they serve the two halves of the same invariant (what gets superseded vs. what gets hidden), a split would cause read/write inconsistency where compact creates a supersedes list excluding a compaction event that the read path WOULD have included (or vice versa). This is a latent maintenance bug that should be unified to a single source of truth.

title: SWEEP-AP: buildKeyProvider and buildThresholdShareProvider defined in create.go but used in join.go
description:
Two builder functions are defined in cmd/cf/cmd/create.go but called from both create.go and join.go:
- buildKeyProvider(cfHome string) CampfireKeyProvider (lines 428-442)
- buildThresholdShareProvider(s *store.Store) ThresholdShareProvider (lines 445-461)

These are not conceptually part of campfire creation — they are infrastructure concerns for the HTTP transport (providing key material to the join handler). They should live in a shared transport helpers file or alongside the HTTP transport initialization code, not in create.go.

The join flow (joinP2PHTTP in join.go lines 267-274) calls both of these, creating an implicit dependency on create.go internals.

Severity: LOW - confusing ownership; a developer reading join.go to understand p2p-http joining must know to look in create.go for these helpers.

title: SWEEP-AP: isCompactionMsg (compact.go) vs isCompactionEvent (store.go) inconsistent implementations
description:
Two functions detect compaction messages but use different tag-matching strategies:
- cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go isCompactionMsg() (line 239): uses strings.Contains(m.Tags, "campfire:compact") — substring match on raw JSON string
- pkg/store/store.go isCompactionEvent() (lines 732-734): uses HasTag() — exact element match via JSON parse

The store.go version was explicitly created as a security fix (workspace-pyw) to prevent false positives from tags like "xycampfire:compact". The compact.go version still uses the vulnerable strings.Contains pattern.

If a message has a tag like "notcampfire:compact" or "xycampfire:compact", isCompactionMsg will falsely identify it as a compaction event, causing it to be excluded from the supersede list during compaction.

This is a correctness bug masquerading as a code duplication issue: the fix in store.go was not propagated to compact.go.

Severity: HIGH - functional bug with security relevance; compact.go could exclude messages from compaction that should be included, or misidentify regular messages.

title: follow-mode uses post-fetch filter instead of SQL filter
description:
One-shot cf read --tag X uses SQL-level filtering (pushed into ListMessages). Follow mode (cf read --follow --tag X) uses post-fetch filterMessages() instead.

Both are correct. Performance diverges for high-volume campfires: follow mode fetches all messages from store then filters in Go, while one-shot mode filters at SQL level.

This is documented as intentional in commit 64ef3a5 ("filterMessages() retained as fallback for follow-mode loop"), but the inconsistency should be resolved when follow mode sees significant traffic.

Fix: push SQL filter into the follow loop ListMessages calls (same pattern as one-shot). Cursor advancement already uses pre-filter unfiltered fetch, so the cursor invariant is safe.

Done condition: cf read --follow --tag X fetches only tag-matching messages from the store, not all messages. Test: add a follow-mode test that asserts filtered-out messages are not fetched (store query count or mock store).

Not a blocker for workspace-o3l.11.
close_reason: Deferred: follow-mode SQL filtering is P2.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleJoin admits any agent with valid signature - no authorization check
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go, handleJoin (line 423-603)

For invite-only campfires, handleJoin never checks whether the joiner is authorized. The handler:
1. Verifies the request is signed by the joiner (line 455-459) - only proves identity
2. Fetches the campfire key and membership record
3. Returns the encrypted campfire private key to ANY joiner who presents a valid signature

There is no check that the joiner's public key appears in an admission list, has been pre-approved, or that the campfire's join_protocol allows open joins.

The join_protocol field is read and returned in the response (line 486), but it is never used to gate access. An "invite-only" campfire will still hand out its private key to any agent that posts a valid join request.

The filesystem transport (joinFilesystem in join.go) correctly checks the join_protocol:
  case "invite-only":
      return fmt.Errorf("campfire %s is invite-only...")

But the P2P HTTP transport handler on the server side has no equivalent check. Any agent that knows a campfire ID and the serving peer's endpoint can join an "invite-only" campfire via the HTTP transport.

Impact: invite-only access control is completely bypassed for P2P HTTP campfires. This is a security vulnerability.

title: printMessagesWithFields projection path always emits trailing blank line

title: SWEEP-AP: campfireStateForRekey phantom duplicate of campfire.CampfireState
description: pkg/transport/http/handler.go defines campfireStateForRekey with identical fields and CBOR tags as pkg/campfire.CampfireState. Comment says it avoids circular deps but if CampfireState gains a field, rekey handler silently loses it during CBOR round-trips. title: Bug: GetMessageByPrefix omits instance column from SELECT and Scan
description:
pkg/store/store.go GetMessageByPrefix (line 250) SELECTs 10 columns but omits `instance`. The Scan on line 262 also does not scan it. This means any MessageRecord returned by GetMessageByPrefix will always have Instance:"" even if the stored message has an instance value.

Fix: Add `instance` to the SELECT list and add `&m.Instance` to the Scan call.

This function is used by `cf read --pull <prefix>` to resolve message IDs by prefix.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 48f36f3. Instance column added to GetMessageByPrefix SELECT and Scan.

title: cf view read does not respect compaction
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.4.2 review.

In view.go runViewRead(): allMsgs, err := s.ListMessages(campfireID, 0) — no RespectCompaction flag. Views evaluate the predicate against ALL messages including those superseded by compaction events.

After a cf compact run, cf view read will still return compacted/superseded messages if they match the predicate. This is inconsistent with default cf read behavior (which excludes superseded messages).

Done condition: runViewRead passes store.MessageFilter{RespectCompaction: true} to ListMessages by default. An optional --all flag on "cf view read" can bypass compaction filtering. Test: view over compacted campfire excludes superseded messages.

Parent: workspace-o3l.4
close_reason: Fixed: view read now passes RespectCompaction:true. Commit c53ed1b.

title: SWEEP-TEST: verifyRequestSignature() - missing edge case tests
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go verifyRequestSignature()

verifyRequestSignature() is the authentication gate for all HTTP endpoints (deliver, sync, poll, membership, join, sign, rekey). The existing tests cover valid signature (TestDeliverAndSync), mismatched signature (TestInvalidSignatureRejected), and missing headers (TestMissingSignatureHeadersRejected). Missing:

- Invalid hex in X-Campfire-Sender header (non-hex chars): hex.DecodeString fails, returns error -> 401 (no test)
- Wrong-length public key (not 32 bytes): fmt.Errorf("invalid public key length") path (no test)
- Invalid base64 in X-Campfire-Signature: base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString fails (no test)
- Empty body vs non-empty body: sync signs empty body, deliver signs full body - cross-endpoint mismatch test (no test)

The wrong-length public key test is P1: if an attacker sends a 0-byte or 33-byte pubkey, the code should reject cleanly, not panic or silently pass.

Done: unit tests for all missing paths in verifyRequestSignature, callable without starting an HTTP server (pure function test).

title: SWEEP-SEC: Threshold DKG shares distributed to all joiners in order — no access control on share quantity
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

During campfire creation with threshold>1 (createP2PHTTP), the creator pre-generates ALL DKG shares for participants 2..N and stores them in pending_threshold_shares. ClaimPendingThresholdShare() is called by handleJoin for each joiner.

There is no limit on how many joiners can claim shares. An attacker who can make multiple join requests (with different keypairs) can:
1. Claim all remaining DKG shares before legitimate members join
2. Legitimate members then receive no share (shareData == nil) and cannot participate in threshold signing
3. After collecting enough shares, attacker has a valid threshold signing quorum

The invite-only bypass (workspace-972) makes this worse: for open-protocol campfires, anyone can join and claim shares.

Additionally: pending_threshold_shares uses participant_id as the PRIMARY KEY with campfire_id — once all pre-generated shares are claimed, no more can be distributed. The creator cannot add new shares without a full rekey.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go ClaimPendingThresholdShare() lines 632-664
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleJoin() lines 534-552

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: BUG: join drops pre-admitted role — observer admitted via 'cf admit' can bypass role enforcement after joining
description:
Severity: critical

In joinFilesystem() (join.go:82-162), when an agent is pre-admitted (alreadyOnDisk=true), the loop reads m.JoinedAt but discards m.Role. The AddMembership call on line 158 always stores Role: "member" (a legacy value EffectiveRole maps to full) regardless of what role was set in the transport member record by 'cf admit --role observer'.

Result: A member admitted as observer via 'cf admit <id> <pubkey> --role observer' — the intended use case for role enforcement — can run 'cf join <id>' and get a local membership record with Role: "member" (= effective full). They can then send messages freely, completely defeating the role system.

The fix is to read the role from the existing member record when alreadyOnDisk=true and propagate it to AddMembership. For the !alreadyOnDisk open-protocol path, default to campfire.RoleFull.

File: cmd/cf/cmd/join.go, lines 88-95 (loop), 154-162 (AddMembership call).

Done condition: joining a campfire where you were pre-admitted as observer results in Role=observer in the local store, and sendFilesystemWithRoleCheck returns a role error for that membership.
close_reason: joinFilesystem() now reads Role from pre-admitted MemberRecord and passes it to AddMembership. Added existingRole variable, populated from transport member scan. autojoin.go gets same fix. Regression test: TestJoinFilesystemPreservesAdmittedRole. Commit: 329703c

title: SWEEP-AP: transport dispatch (isGitHubCampfire/isPeerHTTPCampfire) inlined at every call site
description:
Transport type detection is done by calling isGitHubCampfire() and isPeerHTTPCampfire() inline at every cmd-layer call site rather than using the pkg/transport.ResolveType abstraction that exists for exactly this purpose.

The pattern appears at least in:
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go: if isGitHubCampfire / else if isPeerHTTPCampfire / else
- cmd/cf/cmd/read.go (syncCampfire): same triple if/else
- cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go: switch transport.ResolveType(*m) (correctly uses the pkg abstraction)
- cmd/cf/cmd/admit.go: transportType := transport.ResolveType(*m) (correctly uses abstraction)
- cmd/cf/cmd/member.go: transportType := transport.ResolveType(*m) (correctly uses abstraction)

There are two different dispatch mechanisms in use simultaneously. sendFilesystem, sendGitHub, sendP2PHTTP, and syncCampfire all inline their own transport switching. compact.go and admit.go use the correct pkg abstraction.

This means adding a 4th transport type requires updating each inline dispatch location separately rather than adding a case to one switch.

Severity: HIGH - this is exactly the kind of fragmentation that makes transport extensibility expensive.

title: SWEEP-AP: read.go RunE closure is 300+ lines with multiple transport paths and follow-mode inline
description:
The readCmd RunE closure in cmd/cf/cmd/read.go spans approximately lines 469-708, a 239-line anonymous function that handles:
1. Flag mutual exclusivity checking
2. Identity loading and store opening  
3. Campfire ID resolution (single vs. all campfires)
4. Auto-join for project root
5. Follow mode (infinite poll loop with signal handling, cursor tracking per campfire, sync, filter, print) - ~80 lines inline
6. One-shot mode (sync, cursor computation with unfiltered+filtered double query, output, cursor update)
7. JSON vs. human-readable output branching
8. Description header printing

The follow-mode logic alone (~lines 541-631) is a complete state machine embedded in the closure. It should be extracted to runFollowMode(entries, fieldSet, readAll, readPeek, readTagFilters, readSenderFilter).

The one-shot path double-queries the store (unfiltered for cursor, filtered for display) with inline explanation comments that would be unnecessary if the logic were in a named function.

Severity: MEDIUM - testability and readability; the follow-mode path is effectively untestable in isolation from the rest of the function.

title: Transport compatibility: all P0-P2 features work across fs, GitHub, and P2P HTTP

Current state: tag-filtered reads, roles, compaction, views, and projection reads only work fully on filesystem transport. GitHub and P2P HTTP transports are partially broken.

Scope:

Approach: audit each new feature against each transport. For features that cannot work cross-transport in P1 (e.g. distributed compaction quorum), add explicit transport-type checks with clear error messages instead of silent failures.

title: Transport interface + resolution
description:
Create a unified transport.Transport interface and a resolution function that constructs the right transport from a membership record.

## Design Reference
docs/automaton-substrate-design.md §P2 Implementation Notes

## Interface
```go
// pkg/transport/transport.go
type Transport interface {
    WriteMessage(campfireID string, msg *message.Message) error
    ListMessages(campfireID string) ([]message.Message, error)
    WriteMember(campfireID string, member campfire.MemberRecord) error
    ListMembers(campfireID string) ([]campfire.MemberRecord, error)
    ReadState(campfireID string) (*campfire.CampfireState, error)
}
```

## Resolution
Add `ResolveTransport(m store.Membership) (Transport, error)` that inspects the membership record:
- TransportDir starts with "/" or relative path → filesystem transport
- TransportDir contains "github:" prefix → GitHub transport  
- TransportDir contains "http:" or "https:" prefix → P2P HTTP transport
- Check existing patterns in cmd/cf/cmd/ for how transport type is currently detected (look for JoinProtocol or TransportDir patterns)

## Files to Create
- pkg/transport/transport.go — interface + ResolveTransport
- pkg/transport/transport_test.go — tests for resolution

## Files to Modify  
- Ensure fs.Transport, github.Transport, http transport all satisfy the interface (adapter if needed)

## Constraints
- Do not change existing callers yet — this bead only creates the interface
- Backward compatible — existing code continues to work
- Read existing transport implementations first to understand method signatures

## Test-First
1. Test ResolveTransport returns correct type for each transport_dir pattern
2. Test interface satisfaction for fs transport
3. Implement

title: SWEEP-AP: message→store serialization duplicated in 10+ sites
description:
Every time a message.Message is written to the store, the same 4-line serialization block is copy-pasted: json.Marshal(msg.Tags), json.Marshal(msg.Antecedents), json.Marshal(msg.Provenance), fmt.Sprintf("%x", msg.Sender), then constructing store.MessageRecord.

Affected files (non-exhaustive):
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go:354
- cmd/cf/cmd/read.go:766, 806
- cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go:667
- cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go:213
- pkg/transport/github/transport.go:228
- pkg/transport/http/handler.go:149

This block is duplicated at least 10 times across the codebase (grep: store.MessageRecord{ appears 50 times across 19 files). A helper function like store.MessageRecordFromMessage(cfID string, msg *message.Message) MessageRecord would eliminate this. The pattern is SRP-violating: callers are responsible for knowing the serialization format of the store layer.

Severity: HIGH - every new transport or send path will copy-paste this block again, and any schema change (e.g. adding a new field) must be updated in all 10+ sites.

title: SWEEP-TEST: pkg/transport/http/crypto.go has zero direct test coverage
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/crypto.go

aesGCMEncrypt() and aesGCMDecrypt() are the AES-256-GCM wrappers used for campfire private key encryption in handleJoin and for new DKG share encryption in handleRekey. They are security-critical but have no direct unit tests.

Missing tests:
- aesGCMEncrypt + aesGCMDecrypt round-trip
- aesGCMDecrypt with tampered ciphertext (AEAD auth failure)
- aesGCMDecrypt with truncated data (data too short path)
- aesGCMEncrypt with wrong key length (not 32 bytes) - returns error
- aesGCMDecrypt with wrong key length - returns error
- Two encryptions of same plaintext produce different ciphertexts (random nonce)

These are tested indirectly through the join and rekey integration tests, but there are no unit tests that would catch a regression in the crypto primitives themselves.

Done: test file pkg/transport/http/crypto_test.go covering all paths above.

title: SWEEP-SEC: resolveEndpoint() always emits http:// URLs — no TLS support in p2p-http transport
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

The resolveEndpoint() helper (create.go line 419) always constructs http:// URLs, never https://. The entire p2p-http transport operates over plaintext HTTP.

Consequences:
1. All campfire messages (payload, tags, provenance) are transmitted in plaintext over the network
2. The ECDH key exchange during join (JoinRequest / JoinResponse) is protected by AES-GCM but the outer HTTP transport is unencrypted — MITM attacker can see the X25519 public keys and attempt downgrade or intercept
3. The Ed25519 signatures on requests provide message authenticity but NOT confidentiality
4. Network-level adversaries can observe who is communicating with whom (traffic analysis)

The httpClient in peer.go (line 23) has no TLS configuration. The server (listen.go) calls net.Listen("tcp", ...) — plaintext TCP.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/create.go resolveEndpoint() line 419
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/listen.go listenTCP() — no TLS
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/peer.go httpClient line 23 — no TLS config

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleJoin does not enforce invite-only protocol for p2p-http transport
description:
Severity: CRITICAL

The /campfire/{id}/join HTTP handler (handleJoin in handler.go) accepts join requests and hands out the campfire private key (threshold=1) or DKG share (threshold>1) to ANY requester who:
1. Knows the campfire ID
2. Has a valid Ed25519 keypair (any keypair — not a pre-admitted member)

There is NO check for JoinProtocol == "invite-only". The handler returns membership.JoinProtocol in the response (informational only) but never enforces it.

Contrast: joinFilesystem() in cmd/cf/cmd/join.go correctly enforces the invite-only protocol at the filesystem level. The p2p-http transport has no equivalent enforcement.

Attack path:
1. Discover a campfire ID via beacon (public)
2. Generate a fresh Ed25519 keypair
3. POST /campfire/{id}/join with the new keypair
4. Receive the campfire private key (threshold=1) — full key compromise

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go lines 427-603 (handleJoin)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/join.go lines 105-116 (filesystem enforcement that http lacks)

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-TEST: pkg/encoding/cbor.go has zero test coverage
description:
File: pkg/encoding/cbor.go

The encoding package (Marshal/Unmarshal wrappers over fxamacker/cbor) has no test file at all. It is imported by nearly every package in the codebase (message, campfire, beacon, fs transport, http transport). The init() panic path (bad EncMode/DecMode init) is untested. 

Missing tests:
- Marshal/Unmarshal round-trip for basic types
- Error path: Unmarshal on garbage bytes
- Determinism: same input produces same output (critical for signature correctness — sign bytes must be canonical)

The canonicality of CBOR encoding is load-bearing for signature verification. If encoding produces non-deterministic output or is broken, all signatures fail silently. No test would catch a regression here.

Done condition: a test file pkg/encoding/cbor_test.go exists covering round-trip, error path, and determinism.

title: Follow mode uses post-fetch client-side filtering, one-shot uses SQL push
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.1.4 review.

In read.go --follow loop (line ~558): calls s.ListMessages(e.id, afterTS) without sqlFilter, then filterMessages() post-fetch.
In one-shot mode (line ~624): calls s.ListMessages(cfID, afterTS, sqlFilter) with SQL-level tag/sender filtering.

This dual code path is inconsistent: follow mode always fetches ALL messages and filters in Go; one-shot mode pushes filtering to SQL. For high-volume campfires, follow mode will be much less efficient.

The cursor advance comment explains the intent (pre-filter cursors), but the follow mode never uses SQL filtering at all.

Done condition: Follow mode uses the same SQL filter as one-shot mode. The cursor-advance invariant is preserved by tracking pre-filter cursors separately (as one-shot already does).

Parent: workspace-o3l.1
close_reason: Known: duplicate of workspace-2j0.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleSign round 2 reads signSessions without write lock, races with pruning
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go, handleSign round 2 (line 703-706) and transport.go pruneSignSessions (line 196-203)

In handleSign for round 2:
  h.transport.mu.RLock()
  sessionState, ok := h.transport.signSessions[req.SessionID]
  h.transport.mu.RUnlock()

Then the code uses sessionState.session (ss) WITHOUT holding any lock.

Meanwhile pruneSignSessions (called from getOrCreateSignSession which holds a write lock) and removeSignSession (called at the end of round 2) both modify signSessions under write lock.

The issue: after the RLock is released (line 705), another goroutine could call removeSignSession (from another round-2 handler completing a different session ID) which acquires the write lock and deletes entries from signSessions. The sessionState pointer obtained from the read is still valid (the map entry was a pointer), but ss.session could be used concurrently across multiple HTTP requests for the same sessionID.

If two simultaneous requests hit round 2 for the same sessionID, both will obtain the same session pointer and call ss.ProcessAll() and ss.Deliver() concurrently without any per-session locking. The FROST state machine (state.State) is not documented as thread-safe.

Impact: concurrent round-2 calls for the same session ID (possible in a retry scenario or test parallelism) could corrupt signing state.

title: SWEEP-BUG: compact --before flag accepts prefix but execCompact requires exact ID
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go

The compactCmd flag description says:
  compactCmd.Flags().StringVar(&compactBefore, "before", "", "compact messages before the given message ID prefix")

The flag is documented as accepting a "message ID prefix". However, execCompact (line 127-137) does a direct equality comparison:
  if msg.ID == beforeMsgID { beforeTS = msg.Timestamp; break }

If the user provides a prefix (e.g., "abc123" matching a full UUID like "abc123xx-..."), the comparison msg.ID == beforeMsgID will never match, beforeTS remains 0, and the function returns:
  fmt.Errorf("message not found: %s", beforeMsgID)

The documentation says prefix but the code requires exact match. Either:
1. The flag description is wrong (should say "exact message ID")
2. The code is wrong (should use prefix matching like GetMessageByPrefix)

This is a usability bug: users following the documented behavior of providing a short prefix will always get "message not found" errors.

title: SWEEP-AP: BeaconDir duplicated between cmd/root.go and pkg/beacon/beacon.go
description:
The BeaconDir resolution logic is defined twice with identical behavior:
- cmd/cf/cmd/root.go BeaconDir() (lines 68-77): checks CF_BEACON_DIR env, falls back to ~/.campfire/beacons, falls back to /tmp/campfire/beacons
- pkg/beacon/beacon.go DefaultBeaconDir() (lines 97-106): identical logic, identical fallbacks

The cmd layer defines its own BeaconDir() instead of calling beacon.DefaultBeaconDir(). If the resolution order or fallback path changes in one, it will not change in the other.

Additionally, CFHome() in root.go and the ~/.campfire directory derivation in identity.go both independently compute the home-based default path.

Severity: MEDIUM - config resolution divergence risk.

title: SWEEP-SEC: Role enforcement is client-side only — server accepts campfire:* system messages from any member
description:
Severity: HIGH

role.go comments explicitly acknowledge: "Phase 1: client enforces only. Transport-level enforcement is future work."

The role system (observer/writer/full) is enforced only in the CLI client before sending. The server-side handlers (handleDeliver, syncFromFilesystem, GitHub Poll) store all messages including campfire:* system messages without checking whether the sender has "full" role.

Consequences:
- A "writer" role member can bypass the client check (e.g., direct HTTP POST to /deliver) and inject campfire:compact, campfire:rekey, campfire:member-joined messages
- An "observer" member can send arbitrary messages by bypassing the CLI
- Role is stored in the local SQLite of the admitting node — a different node has no way to verify the sender's role assignment

This is a known limitation but worth tracking as a security bead because the role system is documented as providing access control in the CLI help and docs.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/role.go (client-side only)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleDeliver() — no role check
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/transport.go Poll() — no role check

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-SEC: GitHub transport sends messages in plaintext as GitHub Issue comments — public by default
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

The GitHub transport encodes messages as base64(cbor(msg)) posted as public GitHub Issue comments. The comment encoding is:
  campfire-msg-v1:<base64>

This means:
1. All message payloads, tags, antecedents, sender public keys, and provenance chains are visible to anyone who can see the GitHub repository (public repos = world-readable)
2. The ErrEncryptAtRestNotSupported error in transport.go confirms encryption is not implemented
3. Message metadata (who talks to whom, when, what campfire they are in) is permanently stored in GitHub\s infrastructure

The Config struct has EncryptAtRest bool field but it explicitly returns an error if set to true.

Even for private repos: GitHub tokens have broad scope, repo contents are retained by GitHub, and GitHub staff can read them.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/transport.go lines 18-20 (ErrEncryptAtRestNotSupported)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/message.go EncodeComment() — raw message in comment

title: SWEEP-TEST: campfire.EffectiveRole has no dedicated test
description:
File: pkg/campfire/campfire.go EffectiveRole()

EffectiveRole() is the canonical role normalization function (empty/legacy roles -> full). It is used in checkRoleCanSend (cmd layer) and indirectly tested there, but has no direct unit test in the campfire package.

Missing: direct unit test for each input (observer, writer, full, empty, "member", "creator", unknown).

Done: campfire_test.go adds TestEffectiveRole covering all cases.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleRekey path traversal via TransportDir field — arbitrary file read/write via membership record
description:
Severity: HIGH

handleRekey (handler.go line 938) constructs a file path by concatenating membership.TransportDir with the old campfire ID:

  stateFile := membership.TransportDir + "/" + oldCampfireID + ".cbor"

The TransportDir field comes from the campfire_memberships SQLite table. This field is populated by the joiner during the join process and stored without sanitization.

Attack:
1. Attacker joins a campfire and manipulates their local TransportDir value in the store to contain path traversal sequences: "../../../etc" or similar
2. Attacker triggers a rekey (as the node receiving the rekey)
3. The handler reads from and writes to attacker-controlled paths

Additionally, os.WriteFile is called on a path derived from newCampfireID (line 952):
  newStateFile := membership.TransportDir + "/" + newCampfireID + ".cbor"

Since newCampfireID comes from the attacker-supplied RekeyRequest.NewCampfireID (after hex decode), and TransportDir is locally stored, an attacker with local store access can write arbitrary CBOR to arbitrary paths.

Note: The oldCampfireID in the URL path is controlled by the HTTP client, but the TransportDir comes from the local store (attacker would need local access to manipulate it). Still a defense-in-depth gap.

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go lines 938-956

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

In admit.go: transport := fs.New(fs.DefaultBaseDir()) — always uses filesystem transport, ignores the membership TransportDir. For GitHub or P2P HTTP campfires, there is no admit path. The command will write a member file to the wrong location (or nowhere useful).

title: SWEEP-DEAD: campfire_trust MCP tool advertised but not implemented
description:
The campfire_trust MCP tool is defined in the tools list but has no handler implementation.

Location: cmd/cf-mcp/main.go:388

The tool specification declares campfire_trust for setting/resolving agent display names, but the tools/call dispatcher (lines 1317-1342) lacks a case handler for it.

title: SWEEP-AP: FROST signing protocol duplicated in send.go (thresholdSignHop) and evict.go (buildAndSignRekeyMessage)
description:
The two-round FROST signing protocol is implemented twice, nearly identically:
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go thresholdSignHop() (lines 382-501) 
- cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go buildAndSignRekeyMessage() (lines 429-555)

Both functions:
1. Load DKG share from store
2. Pick co-signers from peer list up to (threshold - 1)
3. Build signerIDs list
4. Call threshold.NewSigningSession
5. Round 1: ss.Start(), cfhttp.SendSignRound to each co-signer, collect all round-1 msgs, Deliver each
6. ss.ProcessAll() for round-2 messages
7. Round 2: cfhttp.SendSignRound to each co-signer, collect all round-2 msgs, Deliver each
8. ss.ProcessAll()
9. select on mySS.Done() with 10-second timeout
10. Extract signature with mySS.Signature()

The near-identical code is ~80 lines duplicated with only the sign input (hop vs. message) different.

Severity: HIGH - FROST protocol bugs must be fixed in both places; the duplication will mask asymmetric bugs.

title: SWEEP-AP: duplicate constant githubTransportPrefix in cmd and pkg layers
description:
The constant githubTransportPrefix = "github:" is defined twice:
- cmd/cf/cmd/github.go:29
- pkg/transport/transport.go:52

Both define the identical string literal. The pkg layer comment even says "Must stay in sync with the cmd-layer constant." This is explicit acknowledgement of a sync dependency that will diverge over time.

The pkg/transport/transport.go package exists specifically to abstract transport type detection (ResolveType function). The cmd layer should import the constant from pkg rather than redeclaring it.

Root cause: the constant was originally defined in cmd, then pkg was added without importing from cmd (possibly to avoid a circular dep). If so, the constant should be moved to pkg/transport and imported from there in cmd.

Severity: MEDIUM - duplicated constants with stated sync requirement; low blast radius for now but guaranteed to diverge.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handlePoll cursor uses ReceivedAt of last record but since param filters by Timestamp
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go, handlePoll (line 337) and Poll client (pkg/transport/http/peer.go line 391)

The poll handler returns X-Campfire-Cursor = ReceivedAt of the last message record:
  cursor = strconv.FormatInt(records[len(records)-1].ReceivedAt, 10)

The Poll client stores this cursor and passes it as the "since" parameter on the next poll:
  url = fmt.Sprintf("%s/campfire/%s/poll?since=%d&timeout=%d", endpoint, campfireID, cursor, timeoutSecs)

But on the server side, handleSync/handlePoll calls ListMessages(campfireID, since) where the since parameter filters messages by TIMESTAMP (the message creation time), not ReceivedAt:
  conditions = []string{"timestamp > ?"}
  args := []any{afterTimestamp}

ReceivedAt is the local store insertion time; Timestamp is the message creation time set by the sender. These are different values. The cursor returned is a ReceivedAt nanosecond timestamp, but it is used as a filter against Timestamp.

Impact: 
1. If a message arrives late (sender clock behind server clock), its Timestamp could be earlier than the cursor, causing the message to be missed on poll even though it arrived after the cursor was set.
2. If the sender has a clock ahead of the server, the cursor could skip messages.
3. The computeInitialCursor function in read.go (line 344) correctly uses ReceivedAt for computing the cursor, but the server filters by Timestamp — so the cursor computed from the local store and the server-side filter are measuring different things.

This is a significant semantic mismatch that can cause message loss in the NAT poll path.

title: SWEEP-SEC: Beacon files world-readable (0755 dir, 0644 files) and beacon signature not verified on load
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

Two issues with the beacon system:

1. Beacon directory published with 0755 permissions (beacon.go Publish() line 111), beacon files at 0644. Beacon files contain campfire IDs, join protocols, reception requirements, transport endpoints, and descriptions. These are world-readable on multi-user systems, leaking campfire topology to any local user.

2. Beacon.Verify() method exists (beacon.go line 76) but is NOT called anywhere in the codebase during beacon loading/scanning. Scan() (lines 148-173) reads and deserializes beacons without calling Verify(). This means:
   - A local attacker can overwrite beacon files with forged beacons pointing to attacker-controlled endpoints
   - lookupBeaconDescription() (join.go line 534) trusts beacon metadata without signature verification
   - joinFilesystem() trusts beacon descriptions from unverified beacon files

Attack: Replace a legitimate beacon file with a forged one pointing to an attacker-controlled p2p-http endpoint. Joining agents connect to the attacker endpoint.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/beacon/beacon.go Publish() line 111 (0755 dir), line 127 (0644 file)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/beacon/beacon.go Scan() — no Verify() call
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/join.go lookupBeaconDescription() — uses unverified beacon data

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-SEC: ECDH key exchange in join/rekey lacks KDF — raw X25519 shared secret used directly as AES-256 key
description:
Severity: HIGH

The aesGCMEncrypt/aesGCMDecrypt functions in crypto.go use the raw X25519 shared secret (32 bytes) directly as the AES-256-GCM key, with no key derivation function (KDF).

X25519 shared secrets are NOT uniformly random — they are points on a curve and may have structure. NIST and RFC 7748 explicitly require that X25519 outputs be passed through a KDF before use as symmetric keys.

Contrast: EncryptToEd25519Key (identity/x25519.go line 173) correctly uses HKDF-SHA256 with salt and info string.

The join and rekey handlers use the raw shared secret:
- handleJoin: aesGCMEncrypt(sharedSecret, privKey) — encrypts campfire private key
- handleRekey: aesGCMDecrypt(sharedSecret, req.EncryptedPrivKey) — decrypts campfire private key
- evictAESGCMEncrypt in evict.go also uses raw shared secret

This weakens the cryptographic security of the key exchange and may be exploitable via small-subgroup attacks or non-uniform key material.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/crypto.go aesGCMEncrypt/aesGCMDecrypt — no KDF
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleJoin() line 523 (raw shared secret)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go evictAESGCMEncrypt() line 639 — no KDF

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-BUG: GitHub transport Stop() does not wait for pollLoop goroutine to exit
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/transport.go, Stop() (line 111-120)

Stop() closes stopCh and sets running=false but does NOT wait for the pollLoop goroutine to exit:

func (t *Transport) Stop() error {
    t.mu.Lock()
    if !t.running { t.mu.Unlock(); return nil }
    close(t.stopCh)
    t.running = false
    t.mu.Unlock()
    return nil
}

pollLoop runs in a goroutine started by Start(). After Stop() returns, the goroutine may still be mid-poll (blocked on a GitHub API HTTP call up to 30 seconds). If the caller then discards the Transport and the store/db gets closed, the goroutine could access a closed db causing a panic or use-after-free.

The goroutine also checks stopCh only at the top of the for loop (case <-t.stopCh: return). If it is in the middle of a Poll call (which itself does multiple API calls), it will not exit until that poll completes.

There is no WaitGroup or done channel to synchronize shutdown. This is a goroutine leak in tests and a potential crash if the Store is closed before the goroutine finishes.

title: SWEEP-BUG: compact double-stores compaction event - sendP2PHTTP already stores locally
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go, execCompact (line 211-228)

The comment on line 212 says:
  // (sendP2PHTTP already stores locally; sendFilesystem and sendGitHub do not -- store here for all paths.)

But then the code unconditionally calls s.AddMessage for ALL transport types including P2P HTTP. Since sendP2PHTTP also calls s.AddMessage internally (send.go line 357), the compaction event is stored TWICE for P2P HTTP campfires.

The AddMessage uses INSERT OR IGNORE so the second insert is silently dropped (returns rows=0, inserted=false). This is not a data corruption issue, but it is a logic error and wastes a DB write on every compact operation for P2P HTTP campfires.

Additionally: the comment says sendFilesystem does not store locally -- but execCompact then stores it. However, sendFilesystem writes to the filesystem transport directory only (not to the SQLite store). The SQLite store is populated during syncFromFilesystem. So compact.go correctly stores to SQLite for filesystem campfires on the same call.

The actual bug: for P2P HTTP, the store write is redundant. Low severity (INSERT OR IGNORE makes it safe) but indicates the comment is misleading and the code does unnecessary work.

title: SWEEP-AP: member join flow copy-pasted across joinFilesystem and autoJoinRootCampfire
description:
The logic for joining a filesystem campfire is nearly identical in two places:
- cmd/cf/cmd/join.go joinFilesystem() (lines 73-184)
- cmd/cf/cmd/autojoin.go autoJoinRootCampfire() (lines 18-107)

Both functions:
1. Call transport.ReadState, transport.ListMembers
2. Check if agent already on disk
3. Conditionally write member record (WriteMember)
4. Conditionally write campfire:member-joined system message (identical code block, same format string, same logic)
5. Call campfireFromState + AddHop (identical)
6. Call transport.WriteMessage (identical)
7. Call s.AddMembership (near-identical)

The only differences are: autoJoin handles open-only protocol, skips invite-only silently; joinFilesystem handles both and also has lookupBeaconDescription. The common core is ~50 lines duplicated verbatim.

Severity: MEDIUM - a bug fix in either path must be applied to both.

title: SWEEP-SEC: Filesystem transport messages not signature-verified on sync; no provenance hop verification on deliver
description:
Severity: HIGH

Two separate verification gaps:

1. syncFromFilesystem() (read.go line 742): reads messages from the filesystem transport and stores them WITHOUT verifying the Ed25519 sender signature. Any agent with write access to the shared /tmp/campfire directory can inject unsigned/forged messages that will be stored and displayed as legitimate.

2. handleDeliver (handler.go line 121): stores received CBOR messages WITHOUT verifying:
   - The inner message.Signature (sender Ed25519 sig over payload/tags/antecedents/timestamp)
   - The provenance hop signatures
   
The HTTP request signature (X-Campfire-Sender + X-Campfire-Signature) only authenticates the transport-layer envelope, not the message content. A member can deliver a message claiming to be from a different sender (crafted msg.Sender ≠ request senderHex) and it will be stored and displayed with the spoofed sender.

Contrast: github transport Poll() correctly calls msg.VerifySignature() before storing (transport.go line 211).

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/read.go syncFromFilesystem() lines 742-780
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleDeliver() lines 141-176 (no VerifySignature call)

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-BUG: RunDKG goroutines may deadlock if routeDKG fails during broadcast
SWEEP-SEC: Campfire private key stored in plaintext in CBOR files; identity private key stored in plaintext JSON
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

Two sensitive key material storage gaps:

1. Agent identity (Ed25519 private key): stored as JSON at ~/.campfire/identity.json with mode 0600. The JSON encoding is human-readable and unencrypted. Anyone with filesystem access (or backup access) gets the agent private key. No passphrase protection.

2. Campfire private key (threshold=1): stored as CBOR at $CF_HOME/campfires/{campfireID}.cbor and in filesystem transport at {transportDir}/campfire.cbor, with mode 0600. Same issue — plaintext private key on disk.

Additionally: the filesystem transport directory uses mode 0755 for subdirectories (members/, messages/) — readable by any user on the system (fs.go lines 47-48). Message payloads, member public keys, and campfire state (including private key) are world-readable in multi-user environments.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/identity/identity.go Save() line 43 (0600 JSON, no encryption)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/fs/fs.go Init() line 47 (0755 directory)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/fs/fs.go atomicWriteCBOR() line 178 (0644 file mode)

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleRekey accepts campfire ID rename from any valid signer (no membership or authorization check)
description:
Severity: CRITICAL

POST /campfire/{old-id}/rekey renames the campfire in ALL local tables and replaces the campfire state file. The handler only checks:
1. That the request body has a valid Ed25519 signature
2. That the node is a member of old-id (GetMembership check at line 847)

But it does NOT verify:
- That the sender has authority to initiate a rekey (only the creator/evicting member should)
- That the RekeyMessageCBOR contains a valid campfire:rekey message signed by the OLD campfire key
- That the EvictedMemberPubkey is actually a member

Attack:
1. Any valid signer who knows an old campfire ID can initiate phase 1 (get an ephemeral key)
2. If they then send phase 2 with arbitrary EncryptedPrivKey, the node replaces its campfire private key
3. Even without phase 2 succeeding, phase 1 stores an ephemeral key in rekeySessions map

More critically: The RekeyMessageCBOR field is stored directly as a message without verifying that it is signed by the OLD campfire key. An attacker can inject arbitrary rekey messages.

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go
- handleRekey: lines 812-1012
- RekeyMessageCBOR stored without verification: lines 988-1009

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleDeliver stores messages without verifying message signature
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go, handleDeliver (line 121-177)

handleDeliver verifies the HTTP request signature (the transport-level signature that the sender signs over the request body), but it does NOT verify the inner message signature (msg.Signature which covers the message content).

The transport-level check on line 135-139 proves the HTTP request came from the keyholder of senderHex. However, the CBOR-decoded message (msg) has its own Ed25519 signature (msg.Signature) over the message ID, payload, tags, antecedents, and timestamp. This signature is computed by the message author (msg.Sender) and is the message's authenticity proof.

A peer could craft a message with:
- msg.Sender = someOtherAgent.PublicKey (forged sender field)  
- msg.Signature = anything (invalid signature)
- Sign the HTTP request with their own key

The message would be stored with a spoofed Sender field. Recipients reading this message would see it appearing to come from someOtherAgent even though someOtherAgent never sent it.

msg.VerifySignature() exists and is called in the GitHub transport Poll() (transport.go line 211). It should also be called here.

Impact: message sender spoofing is possible in the P2P HTTP transport. Any member can make messages appear to come from any other member.

title: SWEEP-TEST: store.ClaimPendingThresholdShare has no tests
description:
File: pkg/store/store.go

ClaimPendingThresholdShare() uses SELECT+DELETE transaction to atomically claim a pending DKG share. StorePendingThresholdShare() stores one. Neither has any tests.

Missing: normal claim, no-share returns nil, multiple shares claim in order, second claim returns nil.

This is called during p2p-http join for threshold>1. If a share is not atomically removed, a second joiner gets the same share (participant ID collision in FROST DKG).

Done: store_test.go adds coverage for both functions.

title: Bug: ListReferencingMessages uses %q formatting in LIKE pattern — antecedent lookup never matches
description:
BUG FINDING from workspace-o3l.7

In pkg/store/store.go ListReferencingMessages():
  pattern := fmt.Sprintf("%%%q%%", messageID)

%q in Go quotes a string with Go string escaping: it wraps in double quotes and escapes special chars. The result is:
  %"some-message-id"%

But the antecedents column contains JSON like: ["some-message-id", "other-id"]

So the LIKE pattern should be: %"some-message-id"% which actually would work for exact UUID-style IDs (no special chars). HOWEVER: %q also escapes chars like \ with \\, and importantly, the % wildcard appears correctly.

Actually on closer inspection: for normal UUIDs with no special chars, %q adds enclosing double-quotes, making the pattern %"uuid"%. A JSON array ["uuid"] contains the substring "uuid" so this WOULD match. But it would also spuriously match messages whose payload (not antecedents) happened to contain the quoted string.

The more important issue: this uses LIKE without ESCAPE clause. If messageID contains %, _, or \, the pattern breaks. Furthermore the outer % wildcards are added after %q, which means for messageID="abc", pattern becomes %"abc"%, which works for JSON ["abc"] but NOT for ["abc","def"] because the contains check is correct.

Actually: re-reading more carefully, this works for finding exact antecedents — the %q adds quotes around the ID, and JSON serializes string IDs with quotes. The pattern %"id"% will match any array containing "id" as an element. This is subtly correct but fragile.

The real bug: no ESCAPE clause means if messageID contains % or _, the query returns wrong results.

File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go:479

Fix: Use json_each for proper array contains semantics (consistent with how tags filtering works):
  SELECT ... FROM messages WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM json_each(antecedents) WHERE value = ?)

Done condition: ListReferencingMessages uses json_each for correct array membership check. Tests added for antecedent IDs containing special characters.
close_reason: Fixed: json_each replaces LIKE for antecedent lookup. Commit e64a658.

title: SWEEP-SEC: SSRF via JoinerEndpoint field — server-side requests initiated to attacker-controlled URLs
description:
Severity: HIGH

The handleJoin handler stores the JoinerEndpoint field from the join request directly into peer_endpoints (line 591-597) and calls transport.AddPeer() (line 597) without validating the URL. Subsequently:

1. The stored endpoint is used in DeliverToAll() to make outbound HTTP POST requests
2. The endpoint is used in NotifyMembership() for membership notifications
3. The endpoint is used in SendSignRound() for threshold signing

An attacker-supplied JoinerEndpoint could be:
- An internal network address (SSRF to metadata services, internal APIs)
- A loopback address targeting the campfire server itself
- A file:// URL (behavior depends on Go\s http.Client handling)
- A non-HTTP protocol URL

The endpoint is also echoed back to other joining members in the Peers list of JoinResponse, which may propagate the malicious URL to other nodes.

No URL scheme validation, host validation, or SSRF protection is present.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleJoin() lines 589-597
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/peer.go DeliverToAll(), NotifyMembership(), SendSignRound()

title: BUG: DB schema DEFAULT 'member' inconsistent with new role constants — stale legacy value in schema
description:
Severity: minor

The campfire_memberships table schema (pkg/store/store.go:18) defines:
  role TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'member'

'member' is a legacy value that EffectiveRole() maps to 'full'. The new canonical roles are observer/writer/full. The schema default should be DEFAULT 'full' to match the canonical constants and avoid storing legacy strings that only work by accident through EffectiveRole's fallback.

This doesn't cause a functional bug today (EffectiveRole handles it), but storing legacy values by default is a maintenance hazard — anyone querying the DB directly will see 'member' and not understand what role it means.

Additionally, join.go, autojoin.go all hardcode Role: "member" and create.go hardcodes Role: "creator" — these should use campfire.RoleFull.

File: pkg/store/store.go:18, cmd/cf/cmd/join.go:158,222,453, cmd/cf/cmd/autojoin.go:95, cmd/cf/cmd/create.go:147,248,391.

Done condition: schema DEFAULT is 'full', all AddMembership call sites use campfire.RoleFull constant (not the "member"/"creator" legacy strings).

title: Tag-filtered reads at store level
description:
P0 — Move tag/sender filtering from post-fetch Go code to SQL WHERE clauses in Store.ListMessages().

## Current State
- `pkg/store/store.go:282` — `ListMessages(campfireID, afterTimestamp)` fetches ALL messages, no tag/sender filtering
- `cmd/cf/cmd/filter.go:14` — `filterMessages()` does post-fetch Go-level filtering
- `cmd/cf/cmd/read.go:431` — calls filterMessages after fetching all messages
- Tags stored as JSON string in `tags` column (e.g. `["status","blocker"]`)

## Target State
- `Store.ListMessages()` accepts optional tag filters and sender filter
- SQL uses `json_each()` to filter tags at query level: `WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM json_each(tags) WHERE value IN (?, ?, ...))`
- Sender filter uses `WHERE sender LIKE ? || %` (case-insensitive via COLLATE NOCASE or LOWER)
- CLI `filterMessages()` still exists as fallback but ListMessages does the heavy lifting
- Cursor advancement still uses pre-filter max timestamp (existing behavior preserved)

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/store/store.go` — extend ListMessages signature, add SQL predicates
- `pkg/store/store_test.go` — test tag and sender filtering at store level
- `cmd/cf/cmd/read.go` — pass tag/sender filters through to store
- `cmd/cf/cmd/dag.go` — pass tag/sender filters through to store

## Constraints
- Backward compatible — no schema migration needed (json_each works on existing data)
- OR semantics for multiple tags (match ANY)
- Empty filters = no filtering (current behavior)
- Do not remove filterMessages — it is used by dag.go and other paths

## Test-First Sequence
1. Write store_test.go tests: ListMessages with tag filter returns only matching; with sender filter; with both; with empty filters returns all
2. Implement SQL changes in store.go
3. Wire CLI to pass filters through
4. Run full test suite — must be green

## Agent Type

model-tier: sonnet

title: Observer/writer/full membership roles with enforcement
description:
P0 — Implement three membership roles (observer, writer, full) with client-side enforcement.

## Current State
- `pkg/store/store.go` — Membership struct has `Role string` field but only stores "creator"/"member" locally
- `pkg/campfire/campfire.go` — Member struct has no role field
- `pkg/transport/fs/fs.go` — MemberRecord is written as CBOR, no role enforcement
- No protocol-level role concept — all members can read and write equally

## Target State
Three roles:
- **observer**: Can read messages only. Cannot send. Client-side enforcement: `cf send` refuses if local membership role is observer.
- **writer**: Can read and send messages. Cannot sign campfire:* system messages (join, compact, view). Client-side enforcement.
- **full**: Existing behavior. Can read, write, and sign system messages. Default for backward compatibility.

Implementation:
1. Add `Role` field to fs transport MemberRecord (CBOR): `Role string` with values "observer", "writer", "full". Default empty = "full" (backward compat).
2. Add `Role` field to `pkg/campfire/campfire.go` Member struct.
3. `cf admit` gets `--role` flag (default "full").
4. `cf send` checks local role before sending — error if observer.
5. `cf send` with campfire:* tags checks role — error if not full.
6. Store membership role tracks the protocol role (update Membership struct).

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/campfire/campfire.go` — add Role to Member
- `pkg/transport/fs/fs.go` — add Role to MemberRecord, WriteMember, ListMembers

title: Compaction events (campfire:compact)
description:
P1 — Implement campfire:compact message type for local compaction.

## Design Reference
docs/automaton-substrate-design.md §P1.3

## Target State
A new `cf compact` command that:
1. Takes a campfire ID and optional message range (default: all messages before a threshold)
2. Creates a CompactionEvent message:
   - tag: "campfire:compact"
   - payload: JSON with `supersedes` (list of message IDs), `summary` (compacted content as bytes), `retention` ("archive"|"discard"), `checkpoint_hash` (SHA-256 Merkle root of superseded message IDs+signatures)
   - antecedents: [ID of last superseded message]
   - Signed by campfire key (requires "full" role)
3. Writes the compaction event as a regular campfire message
4. Store.ListMessages respects compaction: when a compaction event exists, messages in `supersedes` list are excluded from default reads (but still stored)
5. New members joining start from the latest compaction snapshot

Store changes:
- New method: `Store.ListCompactionEvents(campfireID)` — returns compaction messages
- `Store.ListMessages` gains optional `respectCompaction bool` — when true, excludes superseded messages
- Compaction events themselves always appear in message lists

CLI:
- `cf compact <campfire-id>` — creates compaction event (full role required)
- `cf compact <campfire-id> --before <msg-id>` — compact messages before the given message
- `cf read --all` shows all messages including compacted; default read respects compaction

## Files to Create
- `cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go` — compact command
- `cmd/cf/cmd/compact_test.go` — tests

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/store/store.go` — compaction-aware ListMessages, ListCompactionEvents
- `pkg/store/store_test.go` — tests for compaction filtering
- `cmd/cf/cmd/read.go` — wire compaction awareness into default read path
- `cmd/cf/cmd/root.go` — register compact command

## Constraints
- Compaction is append-only — never deletes messages from store
- Compaction is a message like any other (signed, timestamped, tagged)
- Only "full" role members can compact (role check from roles bead)
- Distributed compaction with quorum is out of scope — local only
- checkpoint_hash is SHA-256 of sorted(superseded_id + "|" + signature_hex) for verifiability

## Test-First Sequence
1. Write tests: compact creates valid compaction event; ListMessages with respectCompaction excludes superseded; compaction events always visible; --all ignores compaction; role enforcement
2. Implement store methods
3. Implement compact command
4. Wire into read path
5. Full test suite green

## Dependencies
- Depends on: membership roles bead (needs "full" role enforcement)

## Agent Type

title: Named views (campfire:view) with predicate grammar
description:
P1 — Implement campfire:view message type and view materializer.

## Design Reference
docs/automaton-substrate-design.md §P1.4

## Overview
This is the largest feature. It has two parts:
1. View definition — a campfire:view message that defines a named view (predicate, projection, ordering, limit, refresh strategy)
2. View materializer — reads messages, applies the predicate, computes projections, returns results

## Predicate Grammar
The predicate language must support:
- Tag match: `tag = "memory:standing"`, `tag IN ("memory:standing", "memory:proposed")`
- Sender match: `sender = "<hex>"`
- Time predicates: `timestamp > <unix_nano>`, `timestamp BETWEEN <a> AND <b>`
- Payload field predicates: `payload.confidence = "high"`, `payload.category = "identity"`
- Boolean composition: `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, parentheses
- Numeric computation: `payload.confidence * pow(0.9, sessions_since_reference)` for weight expressions

Implementation approach: Define predicate as a simple expression AST. Parse from a string syntax. Evaluate against message records. Store as JSON in the view definition payload.

Grammar (S-expression style for simplicity and unambiguous parsing):
```
(and (tag "memory:standing") (gt (field "payload.confidence") 0.5))
(or (tag "memory:standing") (tag "memory:anchor"))
(gt (mul (field "payload.confidence") (pow 0.9 (field "payload.sessions_since"))) 0.10)
```

## Target State
- `cf view create <campfire-id> <name> --predicate "<expr>" [--projection "id,tags,payload"] [--ordering "timestamp desc"] [--limit N] [--refresh on-read]`
  - Creates a campfire:view message (requires "full" role)
- `cf view read <campfire-id> <name>`
  - Materializes the view: finds latest campfire:view with that name, applies predicate to all messages, returns filtered/projected/ordered results
- `cf view list <campfire-id>` — lists defined views

## Files to Create
- `pkg/predicate/predicate.go` — AST types, parser, evaluator
- `pkg/predicate/predicate_test.go` — extensive tests for grammar
- `cmd/cf/cmd/view.go` — view create/read/list commands
- `cmd/cf/cmd/view_test.go` — integration tests

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/store/store.go` — helper to list messages matching a predicate (or reuse ListMessages + Go-level predicate eval)
- `cmd/cf/cmd/root.go` — register view command

## Constraints
- View definitions are messages — they live in the campfire like everything else
- Views are campfire:* system messages — require "full" role
- Predicates evaluate in Go (not SQL) — keeps the predicate language portable across transports
- Projection is Go-level field selection on the output
- Ordering: "timestamp asc" (default) or "timestamp desc"
- Refresh strategy stored in view definition but only "on-read" implemented in P1
- Weight computation predicates work on payload JSON fields (messages must have JSON payloads for field access)
- Do not implement "on-write" or "periodic" refresh strategies — store in view definition but error if selected

## Test-First Sequence
1. Write predicate parser tests: tag match, sender match, time predicates, payload field access, boolean composition, numeric computation (mul, pow, gt, lt)
2. Implement predicate AST, parser, evaluator
3. Write view command tests: create stores view message, read materializes, list shows views
4. Implement view commands
5. Full test suite green

## Dependencies
- Depends on: tag-filtered reads (for efficient initial filtering before predicate eval)
- Depends on: membership roles (for "full" role enforcement on view creation)

## Agent Type

title: Security/Bug: isCompactionEvent uses substring match — false positive on tag like 'not-campfire:compact'
description:
SECURITY + BUG FINDING from workspace-o3l.6

Both isCompactionEvent (store.go:375) and isCompactionMsg (compact.go:239) use:
  strings.Contains(m.Tags, `"campfire:compact"`)

This is a JSON string contains check. A tag like "not-campfire:compact" or "xycampfire:compact" would match because the JSON encoding of the tags array would contain the substring "campfire:compact".

While the attack surface is limited (only affects compaction filtering, not auth), a message with tag "bad-campfire:compact-extension" would be incorrectly treated as a compaction event and always shown even when RespectCompaction=true.

Files:

- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go:239 (isCompactionMsg)

Done condition: Both functions use exact array element comparison. Tests verify a tag "xycampfire:compact" does not cause a message to be treated as a compaction event.

title: Spec review: feed P1 design limitations back into automaton-substrate-design.md
description:
Review the 4 design limitations discovered during implementation and update the spec to either:
(a) explicitly scope them as future work with rationale, or
(b) promote them to P2 requirements with acceptance criteria.

Limitations to address:
1. No role change after admission — needs cf member set-role or --force on admit
2. cf admit only works with filesystem transport — GitHub/HTTP admission paths undefined
3. cf compact only works with filesystem transport — cross-transport compaction needs quorum
4. Views are local-only — campfire:view messages not written to transport, invisible to other agents

Also: --follow mode inconsistencies (post-fetch filtering, missing JSON+fields path).

For each, the reviewer should determine: is this a spec gap (the design assumed it would work but didnt specify how) or an intentional phase boundary (P1 = filesystem only, P2 = all transports)?

Update docs/automaton-substrate-design.md accordingly. Add a "P2 — Transport Compatibility" section if needed.

title: SWEEP-TEST: store.UpdateMembershipRole has no tests

- Normal update: role changes and is readable
- Non-existent campfire_id: returns error "membership not found" (rows affected == 0 path)

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleMembership accepts peer list manipulation from any valid signer (no membership check)
description:
Severity: HIGH

POST /campfire/{id}/membership updates the node's in-memory peer list and persistent peer_endpoints table based on an inbound MembershipEvent. The handler verifies the Ed25519 signature but does NOT verify that the sender is a current member of the campfire.

Attack:
- Any actor with a valid keypair can send a "join" event adding themselves or a malicious endpoint
- Any actor can send a "leave" or "evict" event removing legitimate members from the local peer list
- This enables routing manipulation: messages stop being delivered to evicted peers, or are re-routed to attacker-controlled endpoints

This is a confusion between HTTP-level authentication (valid keypair) and authorization (is this agent a campfire member with authority to announce membership changes?).

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go lines 379-421

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-AP: checkRoleCanSend called twice per send with intermediate tags mutation in between
SWEEP-TEST: handleRekey - no test for phase-1-skipped attack (phase 2 without prior phase 1)
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleRekey()

The rekey handler has a two-phase protocol. Phase 2 requires a cached receiver ephemeral key (stored by phase 1). If an attacker sends a phase-2 request without a prior phase-1, the handler returns 400 "no pending rekey session". This is tested in TestRekeyProtocolThreshold1 as a happy-path test, but the following cases are not tested:

Missing tests:
- Phase 2 with no prior phase 1 (storeRekeySession was never called): returns 400 (no test)
- Phase 2 with wrong SenderX25519Pub (key not in cache): returns 400 (no test)
- Phase 2 with invalid encrypted payload: decryption failure path returns 400 (no test)
- Phase 1 with invalid SenderX25519Pub hex: bad hex decode should return 400 (not tested - actually missing validation; currently just stored as-is)
- Rekey to non-member campfire (membership not found): returns 404 (not tested)
- Missing new_campfire_id or sender_x25519_pub fields: returns 400 (not tested)

The phase-1-skipped case and wrong-key case are security-critical: they test that the key cache cannot be polluted or bypassed.

Done: unit tests cover all missing error paths in handleRekey.

title: Antipattern: campfire:* system tag strings are magic literals scattered across 6+ files
description:
ANTIPATTERN FINDING from workspace-o3l.9

System tag strings are hardcoded as magic literals across multiple files with no central constants:
- "campfire:compact" — compact.go:241, store.go:377, store.go:423, read.go (comment)
- "campfire:view" — view.go (multiple places)
- "campfire:member-joined" — join.go, autojoin.go, admit.go
- "campfire:join-request" — join.go
- "campfire:key-delivery" — join.go

This is an antipattern because:
1. A typo in any file silently creates a non-functional tag
2. Renaming a tag requires a grep+replace across all files
3. Tests that check these tags may use different capitalization

The role constants (RoleObserver, RoleWriter, RoleFull) in pkg/campfire/campfire.go are the correct pattern — follow that.

Fix: Add tag constants to pkg/campfire/campfire.go:
  const (
      TagCompact      = "campfire:compact"
      TagView         = "campfire:view"
      TagMemberJoined = "campfire:member-joined"
      TagJoinRequest  = "campfire:join-request"
      TagKeyDelivery  = "campfire:key-delivery"
  )

Replace all magic string literals with these constants.

Done condition: All campfire:* tag values use exported constants. Tests pass. No raw "campfire:" string literals in non-test Go files except for HasPrefix checks in hasSystemTag().
