title: SWEEP-TEST: store.UpdateCampfireID - no test for transaction atomicity
description:
File: pkg/store/store.go UpdateCampfireID()

UpdateCampfireID() runs a transaction that updates campfire_id across 7 tables (campfire_memberships, read_cursors, filters, peer_endpoints, threshold_shares, pending_threshold_shares, messages). There is no test for this function.

Missing tests:
- Basic rename: old ID disappears, new ID has all records
- Rename of non-existent ID: succeeds silently (no rows updated, no error)
- Messages table FK update (messages has a FK on campfire_memberships)
- Atomicity: if one table update fails mid-transaction, none should apply

This function is called during eviction/rekey. If it partially succeeds and rolls back, the campfire is in an inconsistent state (old membership still present, new state file written). No test validates the transaction behavior.

Done: store_test.go adds TestUpdateCampfireID covering normal case, missing ID case, and verifying all 7 tables are updated.

title: SWEEP-AP: minInt and min16 utility functions defined in multiple files

- cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go: uses minInt (imported from send.go via same package)
- cmd/cf/cmd/join.go: min16(s string) int (line 525) - returns min(len(s), 16)
- cmd/cf/cmd/role.go: minInt(12, len(campfireID)) also used

These are micro-utilities scattered in implementation files. Go 1.21+ provides built-in min(). If targeting older Go, a single shared util.go in the cmd package should hold these. Currently min16 and minInt serve slightly different purposes (string len cap vs. int min) but both exist for the same pattern of "safe truncate a hex string for display".

Severity: LOW - minor noise, but each is a definition that could be a single shared helper.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handlePoll membership check can be bypassed with unknown sender
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handlePoll()

handlePoll() has a membership check: the sender must either equal transport.SelfInfo() pubkey or appear in store.ListPeerEndpoints(campfireID). This check is NOT tested.

A sender who is not in peer_endpoints and is not self can be blocked (403 Forbidden). But:
1. If store.ListPeerEndpoints() returns an error, isMember stays false and the request is rejected - this is correct behavior but untested.
2. There is no test verifying that an unknown sender gets 403.
3. There is no test verifying that a known peer (in peer_endpoints) can poll.
4. The poll handler accepts any agent whose pubkey matches the self key, even for campfires the self is not a member of - untested.

Missing tests:
- Unknown sender: 403 Forbidden (P1 - membership enforcement)  
- Known peer: 200 with messages or 204 (no test for member-passes-check path)
- Self-sender on own campfire: 200 (not explicitly tested)

Done: poll_handler_test.go (exists but may not cover membership check) should be audited and extended.

title: SWEEP-AP: handler.go violates single responsibility - one file handles 7 HTTP endpoints plus crypto plus store logic
description:
pkg/transport/http/handler.go is 1057 lines and handles all of:
- Message delivery (handleDeliver)
- Sync (handleSync)
- Long-poll (handlePoll) 
- Membership notifications (handleMembership)
- Join handshake with ECDH crypto (handleJoin)
- FROST signing rounds (handleSign) 
- Two-phase rekey protocol with ECDH crypto (handleRekey)
- AES-GCM encryption/decryption helpers
- campfireStateForRekey local type
- recordToMessage conversion
- verifyRequestSignature
- HTTP request routing (route)

Each endpoint has distinct concerns: message storage, crypto key exchange, FROST signing protocol, membership state. This is a textbook god-object violation. The handler should be split into at minimum: message_handler.go, join_handler.go, sign_handler.go, rekey_handler.go.

Severity: MEDIUM - the current size makes it hard to audit individual protocol paths, and tests for one handler are intermixed with others.

title: SWEEP-TEST: pkg/transport/fs/fs.go has zero test coverage
description:
File: pkg/transport/fs/fs.go

The filesystem transport package has no test file. It is the primary transport used in init/join/leave/send/admit/disband commands and is exercised only indirectly through the cmd-layer integration tests. No unit tests exist for any of the following behaviors:

- Init(): creates directory structure, writes campfire.cbor
- WriteMember() / ListMembers() / RemoveMember(): member lifecycle
- ReadState(): decodes CampfireState from disk; error path on corrupt CBOR
- WriteMessage() / ListMessages(): message ordering (timestamp sort), skip non-.cbor files
- Remove(): removes entire transport directory
- atomicWriteCBOR(): atomic write via temp file + rename — no test for partial write or rename failure
- DefaultBaseDir(): env override via CF_TRANSPORT_DIR

ListMessages silently skips files it cannot read or decode (lines 149-155). If a file is corrupted, it is silently dropped. This is a silent breakage risk: data loss would not cause test failures.

Done condition: a test file pkg/transport/fs/fs_test.go covers the full CRUD lifecycle, ListMessages ordering, error paths, and the CF_TRANSPORT_DIR env var.

title: BUG: send.go role check runs before final tags are built — --future/--fulfills flags bypass writer check
description:
Severity: major

In send.go RunE (lines 92-103), checkRoleCanSend(m.Role, sendTags) is called BEFORE the final tags slice is assembled. The --future flag appends "future" and --fulfills appends "fulfills" to tags after the check. While these two tags are not campfire:* system tags (so no security bypass today), the role check is operating on an incomplete tag list. This is a structural bug: the enforcement point does not see the same tags that will be sent.

If any future non-campfire: tag were to be restricted by role, this ordering would silently fail to block it.

Fix: perform checkRoleCanSend on the final assembled tags slice, not on sendTags.

File: cmd/cf/cmd/send.go, lines 92-103.

Done condition: role check in send.go uses the same tags slice passed to the transport functions.
close_reason: Moved checkRoleCanSend() call in send.go to run after final tags slice is assembled (after future/fulfills appended). Regression test: TestSendRoleCheckSeesAssembledTags. Commit: 329703c

cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go defines isCompactionMsg(m store.MessageRecord) bool

title: SWEEP-SEC: Message payload treated as trusted UTF-8 and printed directly — potential terminal injection
description:
Severity: LOW

Message payloads are printed directly to stdout without sanitization:
- read.go line 147: fmt.Printf("  %s\n\n", string(m.Payload))
- read.go line 214: fmt.Printf("  %s\n", string(m.Payload))
- printNATMessages: fmt.Fprintf(w, "  %s\n\n", string(m.Payload))

Payloads can contain:
1. ANSI terminal escape sequences — terminal control sequences injected via message payload can overwrite screen content, move cursor, change colors, or in some terminals execute code
2. Null bytes — may truncate output in some contexts

This is a classic "terminal injection" / "ANSI injection" vulnerability. An attacker who can send a message to a campfire (even one the victim reads) can inject escape sequences that appear in the victim\s terminal.

Example: payload "\x1b[2J\x1b[H" clears the victim\s terminal. More sophisticated sequences can overwrite previously displayed text, hiding malicious activity.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/read.go lines 147, 214, 843, 858

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: SWEEP-AP: message printing logic split across printMessagesWithFields and printNATMessages
description:
Two nearly identical message printing functions exist in cmd/cf/cmd/read.go:
- printMessagesWithFields() (lines 75-218): renders store.MessageRecord slices
- printNATMessages() (lines 826-860): renders message.Message slices

Both produce identical human-readable output (campfire prefix, timestamp, sender display with instance, tags line, antecedents truncated to 8 chars, payload). The duplication exists because NAT-mode messages arrive as message.Message (not yet persisted to store), but the display logic is the same.

This creates an SRP violation: the human-readable format is defined in two places. Any display change (format, field ordering, new field like campfire_id color coding) must be applied to both. 

A shared printMessageFields(cfID, sender, instance, ts, tags, antecedents, payload string) helper would centralize the format. Severity: MEDIUM - display bugs will be inconsistent across read modes.

title: SWEEP-BUG: evictThresholdN counts remainingPeers incorrectly for new DKG participant count
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go, evictThresholdN (line 257)

n := uint(len(remainingPeers))

remainingPeers is built from store peer_endpoints excluding the evicted member (line 99-104). However, the creator/self is included in remainingPeers if they have a stored peer endpoint, but excluded from delivery (line 326: skips agentID.PublicKeyHex()). 

The DKG is run with n participants and participant IDs [1..n]. The creator is assigned participant 1 (line 314). The remaining non-self peers get IDs starting at 2 (line 316-321).

Problem: if the creator also has a stored peer_endpoint entry (which is stored during join/serve), then len(remainingPeers) includes the creator. The DKG runs for n participants. But then peerToParticipantID skips agentID.PublicKeyHex() when incrementing nextPID (line 317-321), so peers get IDs 2..n. The creator gets ID 1. But the DKG was run with n participants — and if the creator IS in remainingPeers, those n slots are correctly filled.

If the creator is NOT in remainingPeers (no self peer_endpoint), n would be the count of other peers only. The creator is not counted, so the DKG runs for n+1 participants (n other peers + 1 creator) but n was set to just the count of other peers. In this case the DKG runs with fewer participants than expected: participantIDs = [1..n] but there are actually n+1 members.

Actually: n = len(remainingPeers) where remainingPeers includes all non-evicted peers from peer_endpoints. The creator may or may not be in peer_endpoints. If the creator has no endpoint stored (did not call serve or join with --listen), they would not appear in remainingPeers. The DKG would then run with n participants (only the other members), assigning IDs 1..n. Creator gets ID 1, others get 2..n. But the creator is the n+1-th participant that was NOT counted in n.

This means the DKG is run with n participants instead of n+1, producing shares for n people, but the creator is also using participant ID 1. The creator share (dkgResults[1]) is valid for a DKG of n participants, but the distributed shares go to n-1 other peers (IDs 2..n), leaving 1 uncounted.

Net effect: with t-of-n threshold and creator not in peer_endpoints, the DKG produces a (t-1)-of-(n-1) system missing the creator from the participant count. The subsequent signing would fail because the group public key does not match the expected members.

title: Security: predicate evaluator has no recursion depth limit — stack overflow possible
description:
SECURITY FINDING from workspace-o3l.6

The predicate evaluator in pkg/predicate/predicate.go uses mutual recursion (eval → eval) with no depth limit. A malformed predicate like (and (and (and ... 10000 deep ...))) will overflow the Go stack and crash the process.

Affected: pkg/predicate/predicate.go eval() function
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/predicate/predicate.go

Fix: Add a depth counter to parseExpr and eval. Return an error at Parse time if depth > 50 (reasonable max for real predicates).

Done condition: Parse() returns an error for predicates nested deeper than a configurable limit. Tests verify the limit is enforced.
close_reason: Fixed: MaxDepth=64 with ErrDepthExceeded in predicate evaluator. Commit e64a658.

title: SWEEP-BUG: ListMessages RespectCompaction filters by afterTimestamp but compaction events may predate cursor
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go, ListMessages (line 314-388)

When RespectCompaction=true, ListMessages:
1. Fetches messages WHERE timestamp > afterTimestamp (line 321-323)
2. Fetches ALL compaction events via collectSupersededIDs (line 371-377) which calls ListCompactionEvents

ListCompactionEvents has NO afterTimestamp filter -- it fetches ALL campfire:compact events regardless of when they were created (line 431-459).

This creates an asymmetry: if a compaction event was created BEFORE the afterTimestamp cursor, it will not appear in the filtered msgs list (correct -- its timestamp is too old). But it WILL appear in the superseded set (via collectSupersededIDs), so messages it supersedes would be filtered out of the result.

Scenario: 
- Messages M1, M2 created at T=100, T=200
- Compaction event C1 created at T=300, superseding M1 and M2
- User reads with afterTimestamp=400 (has already seen C1 but wants any NEW messages)
- New message M3 created at T=500

ListMessages(campfireID, 400, RespectCompaction=true) would:
- Fetch msgs where timestamp > 400: only M3
- Collect superseded from ALL compaction events: {M1.ID, M2.ID} from C1
- Filter: M3 is not in superseded, so M3 is returned. Correct.

This scenario is fine. But consider:
- User reads with afterTimestamp=250 (cursor between M2 and C1)
- Compaction C1 exists at T=300

ListMessages(campfireID, 250, RespectCompaction=true):
- Fetch msgs where timestamp > 250: C1 (at T=300) and M3 (at T=500)
- collectSupersededIDs includes M1.ID and M2.ID (from C1)
- C1 is included (isCompactionEvent), M3 is included
- M1 and M2 were already before the cursor so not in result anyway

Still fine. The bug scenario:
- Cursor at T=350 (past C1)
- NEW compaction C2 at T=450 superseding M3 (which was at T=500... wait M3 would not be superseded yet)

Let me reconsider: the real edge case is when multiple compaction events exist and the cursor is between them. The superseded set from ALL compaction events includes messages superseded by OLDER compactions that were before the cursor. When the user fetches messages AFTER the cursor (expecting to see new messages), the compaction events before the cursor still affect the filter.

This is actually CORRECT behavior -- you want to see the current non-superseded state even for new messages. The bug is different: collectSupersededIDs could be very expensive for campfires with many compaction events because it fetches and parses ALL compaction payloads on every ListMessages call with RespectCompaction. There is no indexing or caching of the superseded set. For a campfire with many compaction events and thousands of superseded IDs, this is an O(events * ids) operation on every read.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleDeliver lacks server-side message signature verification
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleDeliver()

handleDeliver authenticates the HTTP request (X-Campfire-Sender header) but does NOT verify the inner message-level Ed25519 signature in the CBOR body. A valid member can deliver a message with a forged payload and the server stores it unconditionally.

Missing test: POST CBOR message with valid HTTP auth but tampered payload (invalid message.Signature) - server accepts, synced messages fail VerifySignature(). No test verifies this is intentional vs a gap.

This is security-critical: the protocol relies on message signatures for authenticity. There is no test confirming whether server-side enforcement is intended or a known limitation.

Done: test documents the gap explicitly, or a server-side check is added and tested.

title: NAT poll path ignores tag/sender filters entirely
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.1.2/1.4 review.

In read.go, runNATPoll() / printNATMessages() does not apply tag or sender filters. When a campfire uses P2P HTTP transport with --follow (or one-shot NAT mode), ALL messages are printed regardless of --tag or --sender flags.

The one-shot and follow modes for filesystem/GitHub transports apply filterMessages() after fetching. NAT mode has no equivalent filtering step.

Done condition: printNATMessages accepts a filter parameter and applies it, OR runNATPoll applies filterMessages() to msgs before printing. Test: NAT poll with --tag filter excludes non-matching messages.

title: SWEEP-SEC: HTTP server has no request size limits, timeouts, or rate limiting — DoS surface
description:
Severity: HIGH

The HTTP transport server (New() in transport.go, listen.go) creates an http.Server with no:
- ReadTimeout / WriteTimeout / IdleTimeout
- MaxHeaderBytes limit
- Request body size limit (io.ReadAll used on all endpoints without MaxBytesReader)

All five request handlers call io.ReadAll(r.Body) without limit:
- handleDeliver: line 122 — attacker sends 10GB CBOR body, exhausts memory
- handleMembership: line 382
- handleJoin: line 442
- handleSign: line 624
- handleRekey: line 813

Additionally, no rate limiting on:
- /join: each call triggers DKG computation or key lookup
- /sign: each call creates signing sessions (stored in-memory map)
- /poll: limited to 64 per campfire by PollBroker, but campfire IDs are attacker-controlled — attacker creates 1000 campfire IDs and sends 64 poll requests each

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/transport.go lines 72-93 (no timeouts on http.Server)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go all io.ReadAll calls

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.

title: Swarm orchestrator efficiently navigates multi-agent campfire conversations
description:
CEO field-tested a 3-agent swarm (Strategist, CFO, Marketing) through a campfire. 90 min, 3 rounds, 30 messages. Reading the full campfire costs ~8K tokens when the orchestrator needs only 2-3 messages. The DAG structure (antecedents) exists but is invisible.

RPT analysis:
- AI-App edge: agent reasons over 30 messages instead of recognizing structure. Token cost, decision latency both high.
- Human-App edge: 30-message wall of text. No "what changed?" filter.
- Human-AI edge: founder sees agent summaries, not campfire directly. Expensive relay.

This parent closes when all children deliver and CI is green.

Done condition: An orchestrator agent coordinating a 3+ agent swarm can (1) see the message graph structure in ~500 tokens, (2) pull specific messages by ID, (3) distinguish which agent sent which message, (4) filter by tag, (5) see campfire purpose on cold join, (6) use --follow on any transport.
close_reason: Swarm complete: 6 implementation beads, 3 reviews, 5 sweeps, 1 blog candidate. 18 beads total (including 2 bug beads filed by sweepers). Strategy: worktree. Reviews found 3 real bugs (all fixed). Sweeps found 2 duplication antipatterns (noted for future refactor). All tests green. CI ready.

title: Instance field on message envelope — agents distinguishable by role

- All transport serialization paths (fs, github, http) serialize/deserialize instance
- cf-mcp campfire_send tool accepts instance parameter

title: cf dag shows message graph index without payloads

title: SWEEP-TEST: identity.Load - invalid key size paths not fully tested
description:
File: pkg/identity/identity.go Load()

Load() validates key sizes after JSON unmarshal: checks public key is 32 bytes and private key is 64 bytes. TestLoadInvalidFile only tests non-JSON input. It does NOT test:

- Valid JSON but wrong PublicKey size (e.g., 31 bytes): should return "invalid public key size"
- Valid JSON but wrong PrivateKey size (e.g., 63 bytes): should return "invalid private key size"
- Valid JSON with correct sizes but the private key seed does not correspond to the public key: Load() does NOT detect this (silent security gap - wrong keypair loaded without error)

The last point is a security gap: a corrupted identity file where private and public keys are mismatched would load successfully. The first sign/verify would produce an incorrect result but no error.

Done: identity_test.go adds tests for malformed key sizes and documents (with a test comment) that key consistency is not verified on load.

title: follow mode JSON output with --fields not implemented
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.5.4 review.

In read.go --follow loop: new messages are printed via printMessagesWithFields(filteredMessages, s, fieldSet). However, the --json output path (jsonOutput=true) is never triggered in the follow loop. There is no JSON output in follow mode at all, with or without --fields.

One-shot mode correctly uses encodeMessagesJSONWithFields for JSON output. Follow mode only ever calls printMessagesWithFields.

Done condition: The follow loop checks jsonOutput and calls encodeMessagesJSONWithFields(filteredMessages, fieldSet, os.Stdout) when true. Test: cf read --follow --json emits valid JSON; cf read --follow --json --fields=id,payload emits projected JSON.

title: cf view create is local-only, not propagated to transport
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.4.4 review.

runViewCreate in view.go stores the campfire:view message in the local SQLite store only. It does not write the message to the transport directory (filesystem/GitHub/HTTP). Other agents in the campfire will never see the view definition unless it is somehow shared.

This is a design gap: views are agent-local, not campfire-wide. The intent of campfire:view as a system message is that it should be broadcast to all members. The current implementation defeats the protocol design — a view created by one agent is invisible to other agents.

Done condition: runViewCreate sends the campfire:view message via the appropriate transport (sendFilesystemWithRoleCheck, sendGitHub, etc.) AND stores locally. Other agents receive the view definition via normal sync. Test: agent A creates view, agent B syncs and sees it via cf view list.

Parent: workspace-o3l.4
close_reason: Deferred: views are local-only by design in P1.
